meta.title: Hand Shape and Balance
meta.description: Balanced vs unbalanced hands and why shape matters

blocks.content:
## Shape: The Other Half of Hand Evaluation

You've learned to count points. Now it's time to learn about **shape** — the pattern of how your 13 cards are distributed across the four suits. Shape is described as four numbers that add up to 13, written from longest suit to shortest.

For example:

> ♠ A K 7 4    ♥ Q 9 3    ♦ J 8 5    ♣ K 6 2

This hand has 4 spades, 3 hearts, 3 diamonds, 3 clubs. Its shape is **4-3-3-3**.

> ♠ K Q J 8 5 3    ♥ A 10    ♦ 9    ♣ K 7 4 2

This hand has 6 spades, 4 clubs, 2 hearts, 1 diamond. Its shape is **6-4-2-1**.

The numbers are written from longest to shortest (not by suit order). If you need to specify which suit is which, you write the actual distribution: 6=2=1=4 (spades=hearts=diamonds=clubs).

## Balanced Hands

A hand is **balanced** if it meets ALL of these criteria:

- No void (a suit with 0 cards)
- No singleton (a suit with 1 card)
- At most one doubleton (a suit with 2 cards)

There are exactly three balanced shapes:

| Shape | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **4-3-3-3** | One 4-card suit, three 3-card suits — the flattest possible |
| **4-4-3-2** | Two 4-card suits, one 3-card suit, one doubleton |
| **5-3-3-2** | One 5-card suit, two 3-card suits, one doubleton |

Balanced hands make up about **48%** of all deals. They're the most common type of hand you'll hold.

### Why Balanced Hands Like Notrump

Balanced hands have no extreme length or shortness. They have some strength everywhere, which means:

- You can follow suit in most situations (no voids or singletons to worry about)
- You have some defensive strength in every suit
- You don't have a dominant long suit that needs to be trumps

This makes balanced hands natural candidates for **notrump contracts**. The classic 1NT opening (15-17 HCP, balanced) is one of the most common and descriptive bids in bridge.

## Unbalanced Hands

Everything that isn't balanced is **unbalanced**. This is a broad category that includes:

| Shape Type | Examples | Characteristics |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **One-suited** | 6-3-2-2, 7-3-2-1 | One dominant long suit |
| **Two-suited** | 5-5-2-1, 6-5-1-1, 5-4-3-1 | Two long suits |
| **Three-suited** | 4-4-4-1, 5-4-4-0 | Three suits of 4+ cards, one short suit |
| **Wild** | 7-4-2-0, 8-3-1-1 | Extreme shape |

### Why Unbalanced Hands Like Trump Contracts

Unbalanced hands have one or more long suits and one or more short suits. When you find a trump fit:

- Your long trump suit generates tricks through length
- Your short suit(s) let you ruff opponents' winners
- The combination creates tricks that wouldn't exist in notrump

A highly unbalanced hand like 6-5-1-1 can often take 2-3 more tricks in a suit contract than in notrump, even with the same HCP.

## Shape Determines Bidding Approach

This is one of the most important principles in bridge:

> **Your hand's shape determines HOW you bid. Your point count determines HOW HIGH you bid.**

Here's how shape drives your opening bid strategy:

| Shape | Typical Opening Approach |
|-------|-------------------------|
| Balanced (15-17 HCP) | Open 1NT — the most descriptive single bid |
| Balanced (other ranges) | Open 1 of a suit, plan to rebid notrump |
| One long suit (6+) | Open 1 of that suit, plan to rebid the suit |
| Two long suits | Open 1 of the longer suit (usually) |
| 4-4-4-1 | Open 1 of a suit below the singleton |

We'll cover the details of opening bids in Level 3. For now, the key insight is that balanced and unbalanced hands are bid completely differently.

## Common Shapes and Their Frequency

Here's how often you'll see the most common shapes:

| Shape | Frequency |
|-------|-----------|
| 4-4-3-2 | 21.6% |
| 5-3-3-2 | 15.5% |
| 5-4-3-1 | 12.9% |
| 5-4-2-2 | 10.6% |
| 4-3-3-3 | 10.5% |
| 6-3-2-2 | 5.6% |
| 6-4-2-1 | 4.7% |
| 6-3-3-1 | 3.4% |
| 5-5-2-1 | 3.2% |
| All others | 12.0% |

Notice that 4-4-3-2 is by far the most common shape. Together with 5-3-3-2 and 4-3-3-3, the three balanced shapes account for nearly half of all hands.

Hands with a 7+ card suit are quite rare (under 4% combined), but they're incredibly powerful when they show up.

## Recognizing Shape Quickly

When you pick up a bridge hand, get in the habit of immediately noting:

1. **Your longest suit** — this is likely where you want trumps
2. **Your shortest suit** — this tells you about ruffing potential and defensive weakness
3. **Balanced or unbalanced?** — this determines your entire bidding approach

With practice, you should be able to sort your hand by suit, count your shape (e.g., "5-4-3-1"), count your HCP, and have a complete picture in about 15 seconds.

## Shape and Fit

When you find a trump fit with partner (8+ cards in a suit between you), your combined shape becomes crucial:

- **9-card fit** (e.g., you have 5, partner has 4): Excellent. You can usually take one extra trick compared to an 8-card fit.
- **10-card fit**: Superb. You can often compete at high levels safely.
- **Misfits** (no 8-card fit anywhere): Dangerous. These hands play poorly because neither side can establish a trump suit effectively.

The search for a fit — especially a major-suit fit — is one of the primary goals of the auction.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of these is a balanced hand shape?
reviewQuestions.explanation: 4-4-3-2 is balanced (no void, no singleton, at most one doubleton). The three balanced shapes are 4-3-3-3, 4-4-3-2, and 5-3-3-2.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's one of the big three! 4-3-3-3, 4-4-3-2, and 5-3-3-2 are the balanced shapes.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: 4-4-3-2 is balanced. The rule: no void, no singleton, and at most one doubleton. 5-4-3-1 has a singleton, and 6-3-2-2 has a 6-card suit (unbalanced).

reviewQuestions.question: What kind of contract do balanced hands tend to play best in?
options: A suit contract with a long trump suit
reviewQuestions.explanation: Balanced hands have no extreme shape to exploit in a suit contract. Their even distribution makes them natural for notrump play.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Balanced hands and notrump go hand in hand -- no pun intended.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Balanced hands work best in notrump. Without long suits or shortness, they don't get extra value from having a trump suit.
